title: 🏠 Ending rough sleeping: What the government's new plan means for people sleeping on our streets

content.content: If you're concerned about rough sleeping in your area, the government has announced a new national drive to end long-term rough sleeping at the earliest opportunity, backed by an additional £340 million in funding.
The investment will fund more homes and intensive support for people facing the most severe forms of homelessness, with action beginning immediately ahead of winter.
Here's what this announcement means.
❓ Why is the government taking this action?
The Prime Minister has said that rough sleeping is one of the starkest injustices in the country and rejected the idea that ending it will take decades or cannot be achieved.
The government believes rough sleeping has been driven by years of systemic failure and wants to shift towards earlier intervention and prevention, helping people before they reach crisis point.
💷 What funding has been announced?

The government has announced an additional £340 million to support a new national effort to end rough sleeping. This takes overall funding from this government to end homelessness to a record £3.9 billion.
This funding is the first phase of a five-year programme that will provide homes and support for people experiencing the most severe and long-term homelessness.
🏡 What will the money be used for?
The initial investment will fund:
Work will begin straight away, including acquiring suitable homes and identifying those most in need.
🤝 Who will deliver the programme?
Local leaders will lead delivery of the programme, working alongside central government, councils, the NHS and other public services.
This builds on successful local approaches that have already helped reduce rough sleeping in communities across the country.
⏰ When will people see the changes?

As the Prime Minister said, this government is taking immediate action – with urgent first steps bringing together national and local government to get to work on fixing this.    This will mean we vulnerable people will get vital support before the winter.
Further measures forming part of the wider five-year programme are expected to be announced before the autumn.

description: If you're concerned about rough sleeping in your area, the government has announced a new national drive to end long-term rough sleeping at the earliest opportunity, backed by an additional £340 million in funding.  The investment will fund more homes and intensive support for …

title: Deadline extended for pig sector survey

content.content: Many pig producers and processors are operating in an environment of volatile prices, rising input costs and broader supply chain uncertainty, all of which can place additional strain on commercial relationships.
Against this backdrop, effective communication, mutual understanding and strong working relationships across the supply chain are more important than ever.
Last month, we launched a survey for producers, processors and others working across the industry to share their experiences and reflections.
We recognise that this is a busy time of year, particularly for those who manage both livestock and arable enterprises and are dealing with an earlier-than-usual harvest.
So, to give more people the opportunity to share their views, we have extended the survey deadline to 31 July 2026.
Whether you are a pig farmer, processor, part of a representative organisation, or otherwise involved in the pig sector, we would like to hear from you.
Your feedback is invaluable. It will help us identify where further guidance, communication or engagement may be needed, while providing insight into how the regulations are working in practice.

It will help inform our ongoing work with the sector and discussions with policy colleagues, ensuring that the experiences of those working across the industry are reflected in our understanding of the challenges it faces and future priorities.
The survey takes around 10 minutes to complete.
Please do share it with others working across the sector.
Complete the survey.

description: The pig survey deadline has been extended to 31 July 2026 to give more people the opportunity to participate.

title: Keeping pace? Reviewing benefits, thresholds and caps in a changing economy

content.content: Since joining the Social Security Advisory Committee in September last year, I’ve been hugely impressed by the depth of expertise and the breadth of experience that the Committee benefits from. My colleagues on the Committee don’t just bring a wide range of knowledge, but a range of perspectives too. In all of our meetings I’ve seen a genuine willingness to listen to and understand each other’s views and to reach a shared position that’s all the stronger and more robust for it.
That’s certainly been the case in the Independent Work Programme (IWP) steering group, which oversees the Committee’s programme of research.

In January this year, the IWP steering group met to work through a number of suggestions for research projects, which were submitted by members of the wider Committee. Back then, with so many interesting and thoughtful suggestions of areas where the SSAC could add value with our independent research, it was clear that it was going to be difficult to whittle down the choices. It was so difficult, in fact, that we ended up selecting two projects.
One project is looking at ageing transitions and the other, which I’ve been supporting along with a small sub-group of SSAC colleagues, is on the uprating of benefits.

Uprating helps to protect claimants from rising prices, keeping up with living standards of the wider population, and, in some cases, reduce poverty by increasing benefits faster than inflation, such as through the State Pension triple lock. The current framework, set out in the Social Security Administration Act 1992, requires annual reviews and formal uprating orders. Under that framework, some benefits are required to keep pace at least with inflation, but many are not. Some of these, and some of the rates, thresholds and caps which are important parts of the social security system have either been frozen since they were introduced or are uprated irregularly. These include the Benefit Cap, Local Housing Allowance and Bereavement Support Payment. A key aim of this project was to find out what has happened to these over time. However, as so often happens with research projects, the further we’ve got, the more potential scope has opened up for us to explore other potential connected issues.

It’s been important that we stay disciplined and focused on what we’re setting out to understand from this project – why and how certain benefits are uprated irregularly, what the impact of this has been, and what could be done to make the process better. In our meetings we have often had to remind ourselves of this, and the resource limitations that we’re working under. That being said, we are now getting close to the finish line and publication of what should be a thought-provoking and well evidenced report and set of recommendations.

As well as looking at what’s happened (or not happened) when it comes to benefits uprating, we wanted to understand what that has meant for real people and their lives. So our findings have been informed by interviews and roundtables with stakeholders, as well as a comprehensive database of benefit rates and values that we have been putting together.

We’ve gathered some important insight on where benefits, thresholds or caps have been frozen or uprated sporadically. There is also compelling evidence of how their real terms value has been impacted, and what that means for claimants. Whether it’s inflation, earnings, or the average wages, we’re considering whether benefits, thresholds and allowances have been keeping pace with key metrics. In assessing the impact of this, we’re giving consideration to what the policy intent of the benefit or threshold was in the first place, to consider whether that intent is being fulfilled.

Having set out the problem, we’ve been working together as a group to explore what the solutions might be. Talking through the possible different approaches, we’ve considered whether each is workable, and weighed up the benefits and risks. Whatever the reasons behind the decisions that have been taken, it’s clearly important for us all to understand what process is being followed,  and, as always, we’re interested in ensuring accountability, consistency and fairness.

We’re looking forward to finalising and releasing our report soon, and hope that it will start a useful conversation about the way that benefits uprating is currently handled, and how it might be improved.

description: Since joining the Social Security Advisory Committee in September last year, I’ve been hugely impressed by the depth of expertise and the breadth of experience that the Committee benefits from. My colleagues on the Committee don’t just bring a wide …

title: Natural England publishes Hen Harrier Brood Management Trial

content.content: By Matthew Heard, National Delivery Director, Natural England

Hen Harriers are one of the UK’s rarest birds of prey. However, they are at the centre of a long-running conflict because they prey on red grouse, which are managed for grouse shooting. To help address this problem, Natural England and its partners have explored approaches that could support both Hen Harrier conservation and sustainable grouse shooting. This blog explains the results of the 'brood management' trial, which concluded that this activity contributed to increased numbers of nesting Hen Harriers on some grouse moors, and presents the full evaluation report which has now been published.

Hen Harriers are iconic birds of upland landscapes, yet they remain at the centre of one of England’s most intractable human–wildlife conflicts. Despite being protected by law, they are illegally killed because they are predators of red grouse, and law enforcement alone has not been successful in improving their numbers. Therefore, we have been working with our partners to explore ways of reducing the impact of Hen Harriers on grouse shoots, to support thriving Hen Harrier populations that coexist with sustainable grouse shooting. One method, known as 'brood management', has been trialed in recent years as an avenue to improving tolerance of Hen Harriers on grouse moors.
What is Brood Management?

Brood management reduces Hen Harrier nest density on grouse moors by temporarily removing the eggs and/or broods of chicks under licence, rearing them in captivity, and releasing the young birds back into the wild at a suitable location, once they are able to fly and disperse. Providing a lawful way to reduce predation of nesting Hen Harriers on grouse promotes coexistence between grouse moor interests and Hen Harriers.
The Brood Management Trial – recap of the evidence gathered

Brood management was trialed under licence in northern England between 2018 and 2024, through a partnership of Natural England, the Moorland Association, the International Centre for Birds of Prey, the Game & Wildlife Conservation Trust and the Hawk & Owl Trust.
The final reports of the trial have now been published.
Headline results were published in 2025:
Review of the evidence for brood management

Natural England recognises that brood management is controversial. Concerns include whether brood management addresses the underlying drivers of illegal killing, whether it distracts from the need for effective law enforcement, and whether increases in Hen Harrier numbers are really due to brood management rather than increased monitoring and satellite tagging.

Having carefully considered the available evidence and advice, Natural England's view is that the availability of brood management, including satellite tagging, monitoring and communication of bird locations likely contributed to improved breeding success in some areas.

Current evidence supports use of brood management under licence where projects follow the methods used in the trial. This is likely to include satellite tagging and monitoring, subject to case-by-case assessment. Such projects should be for the explicit aim of increasing Hen Harrier numbers by reducing illegal killing and disturbance.
Not a substitute for tackling wildlife crime

More widely, brood management can be justified as a Hen Harrier conservation tool when used alongside diversionary feeding, engagement, enforcement, monitoring and research. It is not a substitute for directly tackling wildlife crime.
These views are based on the currently available evidence, including the results of the Brood Management Trial, and will be kept under review as new evidence emerges.
Acknowledgements and Further Reading
Natural England would like to thank all the project partners who worked together on making the trial and evaluation possible, as well as the landowners who allowed access for release and monitoring.

description: By Matthew Heard, National Delivery Director, Natural England Hen Harriers are one of the UK’s rarest birds of prey. However, they are at the centre of a long-running conflict because they prey on red grouse, which are managed for grouse …

title: How user research transformed GOV.UK guidance for separating families

content.content: Thousands of people use guidance on child arrangements every month, often at some of the most stressful moments in their lives.
We wanted to find out whether it still met people's needs, and what we could do to improve it.

In September 2024, I formed a content improvement team with another content designer and a user researcher, focused on Private Family Law content on GOV.UK. This is how JDDS approaches digital services more widely, bringing different skills together around a single user problem rather than working in separate lanes.

Our goal was to revamp live content and user journeys based on regular user research. We wanted to understand our users and their needs in detail, while showing the value of improving content that is already live.
Since we formed, we have:

One example shows the impact of this work. Research found that users were often unsure whether mediation was right for their circumstances, which led to confusion and further searching. By restructuring the guidance and explaining the process more plainly, users could better understand their options and what to do next.
Here are the lessons we have learned along the way.
1. Test with scenarios, not opinions
When we first tested live GOV.UK content, users often said it was good, or fine. However, people do not come to GOV.UK to read, they come to do something.

When we set users a task, asking them to do or find something specific, we found vital information was missing. Content can be written in plain English and still fail to meet user needs. Research helps you find out where that is happening.
2. Let evidence decide what to add
Users will usually tell you if something is missing, but they rarely tell you to delete something they do not need.

If you think a piece of content has unmet needs, test it with the gaps still there, unless there is an obvious factual omission such as a missing fee. For everything else, let research show you what is missing, rather than adding more content without evidence it is needed.
3. Good content design works
We do not have to guess what works on GOV.UK. We have an evidence-based style guide and established design patterns to draw on, alongside experienced content designers across government.

Research consistently showed that specific, descriptive headings helped users find information faster and feel more confident navigating the guidance. A good heading does as much to help someone skip what they do not need as it does to help them find what they do.
4. Build a process to get things published

If your team has no process for changing GOV.UK content, start one. Do a content audit to see what is live, then find out who needs to fact check any changes and who maintains the content, whether that is GDS, your own organisation, or someone else.

Explain to policy colleagues and fact checkers what you are doing and why, and keep the group of people involved as small as possible. If you work with GDS, ask about their process and agree a way of working together. Track how long each stage takes, and after each publication, note what worked and what did not.
5. Measure success

Our changes to the mediation chapter increased average engagement time per session by 74 per cent. The usefulness rating rose from 67 per cent to 80 per cent, and the number of users clicking through to find a mediator rose by 14 per cent once we explained how the process works.

Page views and sessions increased, while searches on the page went down, both good signs that people were finding what they needed. Use analytics alongside user research findings to show the value of your changes.
Why this matters beyond family law

This work focused on family law guidance, but it reflects how JDDS approaches digital services more widely. Bringing together user research, multidisciplinary teams, and decisions based on evidence rather than assumption is not specific to one project. It is how we try to make sure any service, across the justice system, continues to meet people's needs long after it first goes live. Continuous improvement only works if it is built into how teams operate day to day, not treated as a one-off fix.
What is next for family law content

We have published a major revamp of the apply for a court order chapter, giving users more information about their options before they go to court. We are adding a help and support chapter so users can find advice from charities and other organisations, and we are continuing to look at the wider journeys people take across GOV.UK.
There will always be more to do, and that is exactly how it should be. Family law content should keep changing, because the people using it do too.

description: Thousands of people use guidance on child arrangements every month, often at some of the most stressful moments in their lives.   We wanted to find out whether it still met people's needs, and what we could do to improve it.  …

title: What I learned when I started teaching agile

content.content: I have spent much of my career using agile ways of working, across organisations and teams of very different shapes and sizes. I have mentored colleagues, sponsored apprentices, and spent more time than I care to admit talking about the value of a well‑run stand-up. Until recently, I thought I had a pretty clear sense of who I was professionally.
Then I signed up for a “Train the Trainer” programme, and something shifted.
This blog is about that shift. It is about what it means to move from practising agile to teaching it, and about what that experience revealed to me about where I want to take my career next.

It also showed me how teaching agile can contribute to wider digital capability across the Civil Service. Foundational agile skills will play an important role as we work towards having 1 in 10 civil servants in digital or cyber roles. I also learned that peer-led training is an effective route to upskilling.
Learning to teach

The ‘Train the Trainer’ programme itself was straightforward in concept. A mixed team of Senior Delivery Managers and colleagues from the Capability team were trained by TPX Impact, a specialist training provider, to deliver ‘Digital and Agile Basics’. This was a full‑day training session designed for DBT colleagues who want to better understand how digital and delivery teams work. The idea was to combine subject matter expertise with training craft.

For me personally, there was another layer to this venture. I wanted to find out whether I could do this. Could I stand up in front of a room and genuinely help people understand something I care about deeply? And, quietly, I wanted to find out whether it was something I would enjoy.
As Delivery Managers, we are already used to explaining complex ideas and facilitating discussions. This made the transition into training feel less unfamiliar than I was expecting.
The experience

The core training materials were developed by TPX Impact. Tash Wilcocks and her colleague, Eva O’Brien walked us through the content during the Train the Trainer sessions. The material itself was well thought through, but a full day of training is never just about slides.
Delegates ask real questions that take you off the slide deck and into the more complex territory of lived experience.

There was one moment that I keep coming back to. The slides provided an overview of different agile methodologies such as Scrum, Kanban and SAFe. One delegate wanted to go further. She did not just want to know what these approaches were. She wanted to understand when you might choose one over another. When would Kanban make sense? When would Scrum be a better fit? What about hybrid or more predictive approaches?
The materials only went so far, so I went off script.

I have worked extensively with Kanban and Scrum, and have developed training on them myself. I was able to talk through real scenarios. For teams with a continuous flow of work and no natural sprint rhythm, Kanban often fits better than Scrum. In highly regulated environments with fixed requirements, a more predictive approach can sometimes be the right tool. At scale, across multiple teams, frameworks like SAFe can help, but they come with overhead. It is important to be honest about whether that structure is actually needed.

I was not reading from a slide. I was talking from experience. The delegate nodded, asked follow-up questions, and was clearly engaged. When my colleague Andy and I debriefed afterwards, he picked out that moment specifically. He could see that something had clicked.
That was when I thought: this is what I love doing. Not just delivering agile but helping people understand how to apply it in their own context.
Upon reflection afterwards, I realised that drawing on experience to help others understand how to apply agile in practice is something delivery professionals do every day.
What the feedback told us

We have now delivered 5 full sessions, and feedback from delegates has been genuinely positive. People said the day felt engaging, that energy levels stayed up across all 6 modules, and that they left feeling they had learned a lot. This is not a given for a full day of training. It shows we did something right.

Delivery Managers spend much of their working lives facilitating. We run workshops, manage complex stakeholder conversations, and help teams navigate difficult discussions. Those skills translate directly into a training environment. Being able to read a room, adjust the pace, and respond to what people need in the moment makes a real difference. Subject matter expertise adds another layer. Together, that combination worked well.

What this tells us is that our experience can be replicated elsewhere in the Civil Service. With the right support from capability colleagues, delivery professionals providing peer-led learning opportunities is not only viable, but valuable.
What I learned about myself

For some time, I have known that I want to move towards agile coaching. That sense crystallised while I was studying for my Project Management Professional qualification, which covers both traditional and agile approaches. During that course, I noticed that while others gravitated towards planning and structure, I kept being drawn towards the people side of the work. How do teams work well together? How do you build trust and psychological safety? How do you create environments where people are able to say honestly when something is not working?
That is the part of the work I find endlessly interesting. It is also what agile, at its best, is really about.

The Train the Trainer programme gave me something concrete to point to. Not a job title, but an experience. I stood up in front of a room, answered unscripted questions, adapted in the moment, and helped people make sense of something complex. That built my confidence more than any qualification on its own and has encouraged me to consider a future in coaching. I have realised that when I teach, I learn.

It also reinforced the value of foundational agile training, not just in theory but in practice. People ask better questions and connect the ideas to their own work when they can engage directly with someone who has experienced it first-hand.
Moving forward

For me, teaching Digital and Agile Basics confirmed that agile training is not just about knowing the methods, but helping others to apply them with confidence. More importantly, it showed me that the skills many of us use every day in delivery roles are exactly the skills needed to start coaching others.

If you work in delivery, using facilitation and communication skills, you may be better prepared for teaching or mentoring than you think. Opportunities like this build capability in others and help clarify what you want from your own career.
If you are considering your career direction, look for ways to share your experience. You might find, as I did, that helping others learn is what helps you move forward.

description: A chance to teach agile became an opportunity for personal growth. Through support, training and practical experience, I discovered new strengths and gained confidence to explore future career possibilities.

title: Consultation opens on five joint UK pelagic FMPs
authors: FMP team, Scottish Government's Marine Directorate

content.content: Today, the Scottish Government is launching a public consultation on five proposed joint UK fisheries management plans (FMPs).
The plans cover:

The FMPs were developed jointly by Defra, the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) in Northern Ireland, the Welsh Government and the Scottish Government which coordinated the work.

The plans bring together evidence about the current state of these pelagic fish stocks and how they are managed. They also set out policies and actions to restore stocks where needed and keep them healthy and sustainable for the future.
Share your views
Read the consultation document and submit your response.
You can respond:
The consultation closes at 11:55pm on 14 October 2026.

description: Have your say on five proposed joint UK fisheries management plans covering herring, mackerel and blue whiting stocks. Submit your response by 14 October 2026.

title: Two Big Milestones for Millions of Care Workers in England

content.content: A message from Rose Thomas-Willis, Deputy Director, Adult Social Care Pay and Careers
These two reforms recognise the vital work that our incredible adult social care workforce do every day and ensure that this work is valued and rewarded in the way it should be.
The Fair Pay Agreement is a landmark reform that will turn the page on decades of low pay and insecurity for the 1.5 million people working in the sector.

Last year, we ran a public consultation of the design of the Fair Pay Agreement process which received over 1,200 responses. We are hugely grateful to everybody who took the time to help us shape the proposals in the consultation, and everybody who took the time to respond to it. Using this wealth of feedback and insight, we have now finalised our proposals for how Fair Pay Agreements will work in England.

We are introducing a regular negotiation process led by a new Adult Social Care Negotiating Body that will be set up by the end of the year. The Body will bring together trade unions and employers to negotiate on behalf of the sector for the first time. It will give care providers and workers voting rights on key issues including pay, terms and conditions, and wider employment matters through a regular negotiation process.

The first Fair Pay Agreement is backed by £500 million in 2028-29. The details of the agreement will be determined by the negotiation process. I It stands to boost domestic recruitment and retention of carers while growing the economy, raising living standards and creating opportunities for all.

Alongside progress on the Fair Pay Agreement, the government is continuing to expand the Care Workforce Pathway- the first ever universal career structure in adult social care. We have added 10 new role categories to this established framework to further support the professionalisation of our vital adult social care workforce by setting out clear roles, responsibilities and development opportunities for all adult social care roles  that are not health or social work, such as catering or maintenance positions.
These two announcements help us strengthen the foundations for the delivery of the government’s mission to create a National Care Service.

I am really proud to have been a part of this work, especially because both policies have involved extensive engagement with the people who know the sector best – employers, workers and unions, amongst others- to ensure we have designed systems that will work for the sector and deliver positive change. As we move into the implementation phase of the work, I am excited to continue this high standard of partnership working.

description: A message from Rose Thomas-Willis, Deputy Director, Adult Social Care Pay and Careers

title: How we're monitoring illegal waste sites and managing environmental risk

content.content: How we investigate illegal waste sites
Waste crime is serious crime. It harms communities, undercuts legitimate waste businesses and can damage the environment.

The Environment Agency investigates illegal waste activity in England. Our role is to assess and manage environmental risks, identify those responsible, and take enforcement action where there’s evidence of offending.
Illegal waste sites vary in size, scale and risk. Some may contain materials that could pollute land, water or air. That’s why we take a risk-based approach to every site.
How we assess risk
When we’re made aware of a suspected illegal waste site, we consider:
Environmental monitoring is not carried out at every site. We use it where there’s a credible risk and where monitoring would help us understand that risk or inform the action we take.

At Kidlington illegal waste site, for example, initial investigations showed potential contamination. We carried out water testing upstream and downstream of the site to help understand whether leachate or run-off could affect the local water environment.
What investigations can involve
Our work at illegal waste sites can include environmental, criminal and regulatory investigations.
This may involve:
In practice, major investigations often run on two tracks: assessing environmental harm and building a case against those responsible.
Taking action against waste crime
Where we have evidence that an offence has been committed, we take action. This may include:
We work with partners including the police, local authorities, fire and rescue services and the UK Health Security Agency.
Human health concerns
We understand that communities living near illegal waste sites may be worried about possible exposure to contaminants.

The Environment Agency’s primary role is to regulate and investigate environmental risks. Our work can also help protect people by reducing the risk of exposure to harmful substances in the environment.

Where we identify information that may be relevant to human health, we share it with the UK Health Security Agency or local public health authorities. They’re responsible for assessing public health risks and providing health advice to communities.
If you’re unwell or worried about your health, contact your GP. In an emergency, call 999 or go to A&E.
Recent reporting about chemicals at waste sites
Recent media reports have referred to chemicals found at several illegal waste sites.

The testing was reported to be carried out on or near waste sites, not where people live. The sampling and analytical methods used provide an indication of the chemicals that might be present. Different testing would be needed to confirm exactly what substances are present, their concentrations and whether they pose a risk.

Waste sites commonly contain chemicals from household or commercial waste, such as cleaning products, old cosmetics or paints. Previous uses of the site may also impact what chemicals are found there.  The presence of chemicals at a waste site does not automatically mean there’s a risk to nearby communities.
We’re reviewing the findings with partners to decide whether any further assessment is needed.
What you should do
Do not enter or approach an illegal waste site. These sites may contain unstable waste, sharp objects, harmful materials, dust, fumes or contaminated water.
If you believe pollution or illegal waste activity is happening, call the Environment Agency’s 24-hour incident hotline on: 0800 80 70 60
You can also report information anonymously to Crimestoppers.

blog: Environment Agency: For homes and habitats.
description: How we investigate illegal waste sites  Waste crime is serious crime. It harms communities, undercuts legitimate waste businesses and can damage the environment.  The Environment Agency investigates illegal waste activity in England. Our role is to assess and manage environmental risks, identify those responsible, …

title: Drought and Water Resource  Information and Links

content.content: Water is a precious resource that we can’t live without; from supplying drinking water and supporting fisheries, to providing an essential resource to business and agriculture, as well as a source of recreation.

It is important that our rivers, lakes, estuaries, coastal areas, wetlands and groundwater are managed properly to ensure the needs of society, the economy and wildlife can be met and maintained over the long-term.
As a result of climate change we are experiencing longer hotter summers and more intense rainfall in winters, leading to an increased likelihood of drought and flooding events.

The Environment Agency has a crucial role as a regulator, in responding to periods of prolonged dry weather and drought. We monitor conditions, protect sensitive habitats, and work with Water Companies and other abstractors to safeguard supplies.
We work with water abstractors to balance the needs of the public, industry and farmers, and the environment, to ensure long-term water resilience.

All sectors should continue to plan and invest for all weather eventualities and future droughts, especially with the ongoing challenge of securing England’s water supply in the face of a growing population, urbanisation and climate change.
We have pulled together some information and links which may be useful to you.
If you have any questions or queries you can call our National Customer Contact Centre for advice on 03708 506 506.
The Environment Agency are carrying out farm visits and will be looking at private water supplies and regulation of taking water from the environment.
If you have a private water supply, how much water are you taking? Do you need a licence?
More information can be found here: https://www.gov.uk/guidance/check-if-you-need-a-licence-to-abstract-water
The Environment Agency is working with the sector to produce Water Resources Management Plans for agriculture to help it achieve improved resilience of supplies.
Water abstraction during prolonged dry weather and drought

Water abstraction is the process of taking water from a natural source, such as a river, lake, stream, spring, groundwater well, borehole, or the sea, for any purpose, such as irrigation, industry, recreation, flood control, or treatment to produce drinking water.

Here is some guidance for non-water company abstraction licence holders affected by prolonged dry weather and drought: https://www.gov.uk/guidance/water-abstraction-flexible-options-in-exceptional-dry-weather
The information on the link above includes:
The National Farmers Union (NFU) have a Dry weather information and advice page:
Water abstraction
Those with abstraction licences are only consented to take water in the pre-agreed terms and conditions.

Abstractions of 20,000 litres (20 cubic metres) per day or less are exempt, provided the abstraction is part of a single operation – if you abstract from the same source at multiple points, the exemption only applies if the combined total of all abstractions is 20 cubic metres or less a day. Any abstraction of water greater than 20 cubic metres from the environment is likely to require a licence.
List of exempt activities which do not require an abstraction licence: https://www.gov.uk/guidance/check-if-you-need-a-licence-to-abstract-water#when-you-do-not-need-a-licence

If there is no abstraction licence, it may be possible to enter into an agreement with an existing licence holder. It can be agreed for a licence holder to give you part or all their abstraction right, permanently or temporarily. This is called the trading of water rights.
More information can be found here:
https://www.gov.uk/guidance/water-management-apply-for-a-water-abstraction-or-impoundment-licence#trading-abstraction-rights
No ‘temporary’ weirs can be constructed to impound water and boost abstraction during low flow periods.
Repairing weirs without consent is either a breach in Environmental Permitting Regulations (EPR) or land drainage consent depending if it’s on a main river or not.
More information can be found here:
Owning a watercourse: https://www.gov.uk/guidance/owning-a-watercourse
Managing your abstraction licence online

You can now use the Manage your Water Abstraction Service on GOV.UK to submit your water abstraction returns online, view a summary of your licence and past returns, and give someone else access to manage your account.

Signing up also allows applicable abstractors to receive Water Abstraction e-Alerts from the service, which are email notifications sent by the Environment Agency when flows are low and licence conditions mean that abstraction needs to reduce or cease. The e-Alerts also let abstractors know that flows have recovered so they can take water again. The alerts improve access to water when it is available, and better protect the environment and other abstractors when it is not.
Details about how to sign up to manage your water abstraction or impoundment licence online can be found here: www.gov.uk/manage-water-abstraction.
If you have any questions, please email WRModernisingRegulation@environment-agency.gov.uk.
More information about e-Alerts can be found here: https://defradigital.blog.gov.uk/2022/08/23/preservation-compliance-and-registration-three-keys-to-managing-our-precious-water-resources/
Spray irrigation

Sprinkler/spray irrigation is the method of applying water to a controlled manner in that it is similar to rainfall. The water is distributed through a network that may include pumps, valves, pipes, and sprinklers.
Spray irrigation abstraction licence holders should have a number of actions to ensure the efficient use of water on the farm.
Many of these measures represent best practice and will help to make the most of the water which the licence authorises to take.

Information on how to manage water use, levels, drainage and irrigation, and avoid pollution from waste water and sheep dip, can be found here: https://www.gov.uk/guidance/manage-water-on-land-guidance-for-land-managers
Environment Agency updated prospects for irrigation - forecast for 2023, can be found here: https://www.farmingadviceservice.org.uk/technical-articles
Farming Rules for Water
Any new water sources would also need to comply with the new farming rules for water regarding livestock and protecting water sources.
Details of the ‘farming rules for water’ can be found here: https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/farming-rules-for-water-in-england
Horticulture, water infrastructure and grants

The Department for the Environment, Food & Rural Affairs (Defra) is supporting farmers and growers to boost horticultural production by providing targeted productivity grants, such as those through the Farming Investment Fund and Farming Innovation Programme.
For long-term planning of on-farm water infrastructure, such as water reservoirs and new irrigation systems, the Rural Payments Agency launched the Water Management grant.
More information can be found here:
Farming Equipment and Technology Fund
How to apply for a local water resources options screening study
Reporting incidents
If you are out and about please report incidents to our hotline 0800 80 70 60.
It's free and open 24 hours a day.
Incidents you should report if you see them include:
More information on other incidents we would like to know about and how to report can be found here: https://www.gov.uk/report-an-environmental-incident
If you have any questions or queries you can call our National Customer Contact Centre for advice on 03708 506 506.

blog: Environment Agency: For homes and habitats.
description: Water is a precious resource that we can’t live without; from supplying drinking water and supporting fisheries, to providing an essential resource to business and agriculture, as well as a source of recreation. It is important that our rivers, lakes, …

title: What I heard from more than 150 SME leaders – and what Skills England will do next, by Anthony Impey

content.content: Earlier this year, I spent a week travelling across the country and speaking with over 150 leaders of small and medium sized enterprises (SMEs).
The conversations took place in different places and sectors, but a consistent picture emerged.
There is no shortage of ambition among smaller businesses. Leaders want to grow, invest in their people, improve how their businesses operate and make better use of new technology.
The challenge is that they are trying to do all of that while running the business day to day.

Costs are up. Margins are tight. Recruitment remains difficult. For many smaller firms, the person thinking about the next stage of growth is also dealing with customers, managing the team and keeping the business moving.
What I’ve heard from across the country
The clearest message I took from these conversations was that, for many SME leaders, the immediate constraint is not ambition. It is time and headspace.

Many business leaders already know that investing in skills matters. They can see the link between stronger teams, better performance and long-term growth. But too often the skills system can feel difficult to navigate, slow to engage, and disconnected from the practical realities of running a business.

That matters. SMEs provide three-fifths of private-sector jobs and sit at the heart of communities and local economies across the country. Their ability to develop people and create opportunity will be central to achieving better jobs, stronger growth and higher productivity.
The conversations pointed to three practical priorities.
Make it easier for SMEs to tap into the skills system
Smaller businesses should not need to become experts in the skills system before they can develop their people.

They need clear information about the options available, where to go for support, and what is likely to work for a business of their size and sector. That means reaching them through the people and organisations they already trust, including their accountants, trade bodies and local business networks.
It also means giving them access to other business leaders who can talk about the difference that investing in skills has made to their own business.
Help SMEs work together to get more from the skills system
A single small business may not have enough demand to create a training cohort, commission a tailored programme or establish a meaningful talent pipeline.
But businesses working together can achieve far more.

Stronger local and sector-based skills ecosystems can bring employers, colleges, providers and other partners together around shared needs. I have seen examples of this already happening around the country in sectors as varied as defence, construction, gaming and textiles.
These partnerships can help smaller businesses pool demand, share insight, and access training, talent pipelines and support that would be difficult to get on their own.
Make the business case for investing in skills clearer

Smaller employers do not need to be persuaded that people matter. They need a clearer view of how investing in skills can help solve the challenges in front of them: driving growth, improving productivity, managing rising costs, filling roles, retaining good people, adopting new technology and creating opportunities for progression.
Moving forward
This is why Skills England is establishing three Task and Finish groups, focused on each of these priorities.

The groups will bring together SME leaders, subject-matter experts and partners from across the skills system. Their purpose will be to turn real business experience into practical approaches that can be tested, refined and, where they work, scaled.
The focus will be on the barriers that matter most to smaller businesses, and on changes that can make a real difference.

As a Skills England board member, I want to ensure that the experience of smaller businesses remains at the heart of this work. That means bringing employer insight in early, learning from what is already working in different places and being clear about where the system still needs to get better.
Small business leaders do not need to be told that skills matter. They already understand the value of a capable, motivated team.
The challenge is making it easier to act on that understanding, without adding another thing to an already crowded to-do list.
Anthony Impey
Skills England board member

description: Earlier this year, I spent a week travelling across the country and speaking with over 150 leaders of small and medium sized enterprises (SMEs). The conversations took place in different places and sectors, but a consistent picture emerged. There is …

title: Existing medicines and new uses
authors: Ian Rogers - Head of Health and Social Care

content.content: Across the health and care system, there is continued interest in how to make the best use of existing medicines. One area attracting increasing attention is drug repurposing – using established medicines to treat conditions other than those for which they were originally licensed.
The concept is not new, and it continues to be discussed in the context of efforts to improve outcomes and make effective use of NHS resources.
In this blog, we explore the role of repurposed medicines within the wider landscape of innovation and system efficiency. We consider how actuarial analysis can support decision-making in this space.
The current landscape

Discussions have focused on how repurposed medicines might contribute to improved patient outcomes and better value for the NHS. This interest is consistent with the intention set out in the government’s 10‑Year Health Plan for England. Its aim is to “put the NHS on a sustainable footing by adopting a new value‑based approach, that aligns resources to achieve better health outcomes”.
Alongside academic and clinical interest, there has been wider discussion of the practical considerations involved in bringing repurposed medicines into routine use.

National Institute for Health and Care Excellence appraisal and guidance processes apply to both new and existing treatments. They place evidential standards at the centre of NHS treatment adoption decisions.

In April 2025, NHS England concluded that a central programme was not the most effective mechanism for supporting medicines repurposing. It therefore decided to suspend its national medicines repurposing programme.
The reasons cited were primarily that:
That decision does not remove the wider interest in repurposing, or the potential value of considering where existing medicines might improve outcomes or support better use of NHS resources.

Opportunities for repurposing can still be identified and taken forward through other routes, such as clinical off-label use, research projects and NHS commissioning decisions. Research funders, including the Medical Research Council, also support repurposing projects through established funding routes.

Regulatory considerations shape how repurposed medicines are used in practice. In some cases, use falls outside a medicine’s licence, meaning prescribing decisions rest with clinicians and liability shifts accordingly. This can influence the pace and extent of adoption.
Why repurposing matters
Repurposed medicines can offer advantages:

Realising these benefits depends on several factors. These include the strength of the clinical evidence base, regulatory pathways, and the feasibility of generating sufficient evidence to support adoption at scale. For generic medicines, incentives to generate this evidence may be limited, as the costs of further research may not be matched by sufficient commercial returns.
Addressing these factors could help unlock benefits that align with broader ambitions to support innovation, improve population health outcomes, and build system resilience.
An actuarial perspective

Actuaries have long been involved in helping public bodies make long-term decisions under uncertainty. This is particularly relevant where costs, risks and outcomes interact across complex systems. Repurposed medicines present an opportunity to apply this lens in several ways:
1. Exploring uncertainty

Actuaries can support decision makers by analysing clinical, behavioural and financial uncertainties – for example, around effectiveness, the strength of available evidence, uptake rates, duration of benefit and potential system impacts. They can also structure alternative scenarios and test the sensitivity of conclusions to different assumptions.
2. Quantifying potential system impacts

Repurposed medicines may, in some scenarios, reduce avoidable morbidity or death. Where appropriate data is available, actuarial modelling can help quantify these potential pathways, drawing on published studies, routinely collected health information or emerging research. When evidence is limited, scenario analysis can still support informed discussion.
3. Understanding population-level effects

Well-established public health research demonstrates that even modest improvements in individual outcomes can lead to meaningful population-level benefits over time. Examples include reductions in blood pressure or improved management of chronic conditions. Actuarial approaches help assess how such effects may accumulate across different groups.
4. Supporting value-for-money assessments

By bringing together costs, health outcomes and wider system implications, actuarial analysis can complement economic approaches. It can help decision-makers understand a range of possible outcomes across different scenarios.
Looking ahead
As interest in the potential role of repurposed medicines continues, careful assessment of evidence, uncertainty and system impacts will remain central to decision-making.
Analytical approaches can help place repurposing alongside other options, supporting more consistent prioritisation within a complex and resource-constrained system.

Actuarial analysis can support a structured, objective approach to understanding where repurposed medicines may add value. By modelling long-term outcomes, analysing uncertainty, and considering cross-system impacts, GAD can help public bodies assess opportunities within a robust and evidence-informed framework.
Disclaimer
The views expressed are the author’s own and the opinions in this blog post are not intended to provide specific advice. For our full disclaimer, please see the About this blog page.

description: An overview of medicines repurposing, the challenges of generating evidence, and how actuarial analysis can support value‑based decision‑making.

title: Countryside Stewardship Higher Tier: express your interest

content.content: From today, we're introducing a new Expression of Interest (EOI) process as part of our approach to widen participation in Countryside Stewardship Higher Tier (CSHT).
You can submit an EOI for:
You can also submit an EOI for new single-focus agreements, which are designed to support specific environmental priorities in the following areas:
Through single-focus agreements, you can access funding through a simpler route. These agreements are designed to reduce complexity and make it easier and quicker to join the scheme.
To allow applications to be processed efficiently, up to 1,200 single-focus agreements will initially be available.

Depending on the features and characteristics of your land, you may wish to have more than one single-focus agreement. This is possible, just note that you must complete a separate EOI form for each agreement.
Getting started
To express your interest, you must complete an online form. Before you do, read the guidance for CSHT on GOV.UK.
Take a moment to check that your business email address and permissions are up to date.

If you're completing the EOI on someone else's behalf, please make sure you have the correct permissions to act for their business so they can be verified. This will help ensure your EOI is processed as smoothly as possible.
Complete the online EOI form for CSHT.
You will need your:
You’ll be asked a range of questions and, after submitting, you’ll be given the option to have a copy of it emailed to you.
Submitting an EOI form doesn't guarantee that you'll be offered a CSHT agreement.
You will be contacted within 10 weeks of submitting your EOI with the outcome.
Contact will be made using the main email address registered on the Rural Payments service.
Subscribe to the Farming Blog
Over the course of the year, we will continue to expand access to CSHT.
By subscribing to the Farming Blog you'll be the first to receive updates like this, alongside information on other support available to farmers and land managers in England.

description: From today, you can express your interest in a Countryside Stewardship Higher Tier (CSHT) agreement for agroforestry or woodland improvement. You can also express your interest in single-focus agreements for scheduled monument management or species-rich grassland.

title: How climate change is impacting the way flooding is managed

content.content: At the Flood & Coast conference 2026, Environment Agency Chief Executive Philip Duffy spoke about climate challenges and what they mean for those working in flooding, the new approach to delivering flood defences and how we need to work together to build systems and capability to deliver on the ambitions the country has for flood defence in the years ahead. In this blog post, he focuses on the main points of the speech.
I've been at the Environment Agency for three years, and during that time the UK, in common with many places around the world, has experienced some decidedly unusual weather.

In January 2024, we were hit by Storm Henk. The Avon, the Thames, the Trent, and the Severn were all exceeding record levels in the same week. In September 2024, the East Midlands had 300% of its long-term rainfall. In the northwest of England, the total rainfall on New Year's Day 2025 was 90 millimetres, recorded at Denton. That's the kind of rainfall that would not embarrass a tropical island, and to experience that here in England's northwest in January tells you just how fast the scale of the challenge is changing. On that day, the Mersey was an incredible 3.2 metres above its record ever level at Brinksway.
It isn't just the rain that's breaking records month after month. We've just recorded the highest ever temperatures consecutively in May in England at 35.1 degrees centigrade.

All of that only really illustrates what the scientists are saying unequivocally about the state of our nation's climate: our weather is becoming more volatile, it's becoming more extreme. And we can see a pattern occurring — wetter winters, hotter and drier summers, and when the rain comes, heavier and more destructive rainfall.

That volatility brings real-world consequences: rising flood risk in communities, disruption to infrastructure and to agriculture, and growing pressure on the systems we rely upon every day. And perhaps the hardest thing for people like me trying to communicate these risks is that the changes we are already seeing are only the start, with a great deal more to come.
Assessing future flood risk

Last year was a milestone for the Environment Agency in that we published our latest national flood risk assessment — NAFRA 2. It enabled us to use more up-to-date climate science to quantify flood risk down to two square metres right across England. It shows that 6.3 million houses in England are already at risk of flooding, and that will rise to 8 million by the middle of the century. The biggest element of that risk is not rivers, and it is not the coast. It is sudden surface water flooding caused by summer downpours. And those risks apply in places where people do not know that they're at risk and where they don't necessarily know what to do should that risk materialise.

It's not just about homes. 59% of our grade 1 agricultural land across England is at risk of flooding. Around half our rail network is at flood risk and a third of our road network, and the economic consequences for us of not taking that seriously could be extremely severe in the years ahead.
How are flood defences changing?

So that shift in risk, that move to a different form of climate risk and a different form of flood risk, means we need different responses as a country. Last year, following the spending review, the Treasury committed £7.9 billion to a 10-year pipeline of capital investment to maintain existing defences and invest in new defences, nature-based solutions, and property-level flood resilience. Our aim in that investment is to protect a further 840,000 homes by 2035–36.

Since 2015, 455,000 homes across England have had improved flood defences, thanks to the work of not just the EA, but many other authorities, and 62,000 of those improvements were in the last two years.

Much of the money committed will go on new defences, and we have major schemes in preparation, notably along the Thames, which has the largest undefended floodplain in Europe, but also the completion of existing in-flight schemes like the Bridgewater Barrier.
The new funding policy from Defra makes our approach much broader than just new defences. It allows us to do greater reconditioning and replacement of existing defences which are life-expired.
Warning and informing

We do sadly have to recognise that flooding will remain a real problem for many communities in the years ahead. That is why warning, informing, and resilience are just as important a part of our response as building defences. Last year, the Environment Agency issued 3,000 flood alerts and 900 of the more severe flood warnings. We have 1.54 million people signed up to our new, completely free, flood warning system. And we're spending £2.9 million in the coming year to complete improvements in our modelling to make sure we're keeping up with changes in climate and rainfall.

But to warn is only half the issue. Communities need clear, actionable information, practical support, and confidence, if they're to recover from flooding rapidly. Property Flood Resilience is one tool we can use to help this, helping individuals and communities adapt their properties to recover more quickly. In Devon, the EA now has new forecasting systems which give earlier warning of surface water flooding.
Water as a single system

The more I spend time thinking about flooding and water quality and pollution, the more convinced I've become that you've got to think about water as a single system, particularly at the catchment level. Key to that will be a shift towards more regional systems planning, thinking about how water planning, catchment management, and investment come together at a regional tier to deliver fantastic outcomes.

We are not passive observers of what's going on either in our climate or our communities. We have the power and influence to make that difference — and if we can work together, from the voluntary sector, from the supply chain, from the public authorities, from the regulators, and from our colleagues in government, we really can make an enormous difference.

blog: Environment Agency: For homes and habitats.
